texts.orig: An attempt was made in this study to prepare very thin palladium membranes. Pd was electrolessly deposited on the inner side surface of a mesoporous ceramic tube using a peristaltic pump to circulate the electroless solution through the tube. To obtain defect-free Pd membranes, a two-step deposition procedure from a dilute P d 2 ' bath and a concentrated Pd 2 ' bath was practised with great care to avoid the difficulties associated with the mismatch of thermal expansion coefficients between Pd and the ceramic substrate. The various membranes produced were characterized through a study of their permeation behaviors. A dense Pd membrane about 2 pm thick was permselective to hydrogen.  It was found that in this very thin Pd membrane case, the hydrogen permeation  flux was better correlated to hydrogen pressure using a power higher than 0.5, showing deviation from Sieverts' law. To evaluate the efficiency of the prepared thin Pdlceramic membranes, iso-butane dehydrogenation was used as a model reaction. Enhanced dehydrogenation yields were obtained in the membrane reactor due to the separation of hydrogen from the reaction medium.

texts.orig: Le present article porte sur la preparation  de membranes de palladium ultraminces. Le depdt de palladium  a ete fait par placage sans electrode sur la paroi interieure d'un tube mesoporeux de ceramique avec I'aide d'un pompe peristaltique qui fait circuler la solution de deposition a travers le tube. Pour obtenir des membranes composites sans defaut, le depdt a bte effectue en deux etapes. Dans la premiere etape, un bain de Pd2' dilue a ete utilise pour deposer des particules isolees de Pd sur la surface du substrat. Dans la deuxieme etape, un film de Pd continu est forme dans un bain de Pd 2 ' concentre. I1 faut mettre beaucoup de le depbt pour surmonter les difficultes associees a la grosse difference des coefficients de dilatation thermique entre le Pd et le substrat ceramique. Diverses membranes preparees ont ete caracterisees par I'etude de leur comportement a la permeation des gaz. Une membrane non-poreuse d'une epaisseur de 2 p m n'etait permeable qu'a I'hydrogene. Dans ce cas-la, le flux de permeation  d'hydrogene  est relie a la pression d'hydrogene a un  exposant superieur a 0.5. Le deshydrogenation  de I'isobutane a ete choisie comme reaction  modble pour evaluer les membranes ainsi preparees. Les rendements de la reaction de deshydrogenation ont ete plus eleves dans le reacteur a membrane du fait de la separation de I'hydrogene.

texts.orig: n  recent  years,  interest  in  the  development of  catalytic I membranes has been  rapidly  growing and  has  brought new  perspectives  in  catalysis and  separation  engineering (Armor, 1992; Shu et al., 1991;  Zaman and Chakma, 1994). It  has  been  shown that the use of membrane reactors can shift an equilibrium-limited  reaction toward the product side by selective separation of some reaction products, typically hydrogen. The removal of product hydrogen from the reaction medium thus enhances the reaction conversion. To date, major efforts were focused on the preparation of membranes with high gas permeability, selectivity and mechanical stability. Composite  membranes were often suggested in which a mechanically resistant porous support is covered w i t h a very thin permselective top  layer. Various types of  membranes were reported in spite of the fact that several technical problems  must  be  still  solved  before  industrial  application (Saracco et al., 1994;  Armor, 1995).

texts.orig: micropores as small as 3 to 4 nm were produced by the solgel deposition technique (Zaspalis et al., 1991a;  Julbe et al., 1993).  Gas  permeation  through  these  membranes  often drops  into  Knudsen  difision  regime,  showing  a  limited permselectivity. Micropores of about 2 nm were also prepared  in  the  case  of  a  silica  membrane  (Ioannides  and Gavalas, 1993).  It seems difficult to obtain micropores less than 2 nm using the sol-gel method in order to achieve further  molecular  sieving separation in  the  case  of  ceramic membranes. An alternative candidate is  the  zeolite membrane, in which synthesized  zeolite crystals fill in the pores of a ceramic substrate. In the last case, molecules permeate through the microchannels of  the  zeolite  lattice, typically 0. 5 - 0 . 6 nm in diameter in the case of silicalite, leading to molecular sieving according to the kinetic diameters of molecules. High gas permeation selectivities  have therefore  been achieved (Uzio et al., 1994).  It should be pointed out that in the case of either microporous ceramic membranes or zeolite membranes, as a rule, gas separation selectivity  increases at the expense of permeability as the pores get smaller.

texts.orig: genation.  These  materials  are  excellent  as  well  in  their hydrogen permselectivity, but the first Pd membranes were made of metallic foils, the thickness of which greatly limits the H2 permeation capacity.  To overcome this limitation and also enhance the mechanical strength, Pd-based films were supported  on  such  porous  substrates as  porous  ceramics (Deng and Wu, 1994;  Kikuchi, 1995),  porous glass (Uemiya et  al., 1991a,b), porous  stainless steel (Shu  et  al., 1993, 1994a,b, 1995). Relatively thick Pd  films  around  20 pn were  often reported. These membranes were basically prepared  by  means  of  electroless plating.  The  use  of  thick membranes not only limits the hydrogen permeation capacity,  but also increases  the investment level. According to the process evaluation made by Mordkovich et al. (1992), the investment of a Pd membrane process is almost proportional to the thickness of Pd films. Thus, the technique to prepare thin but dense Pd-based membranes is of importance to the development of Pd-based membrane processes. Collins and Way (1993) carried out the deposition of palladium film on ceramic membranes having pore diameters of  10-200  nm, and found that a Pd film less than 10 pn thick was still permeable to nitrogen at ambient temperature. Ye et al. (1991) described briefly the preparation of an ultrathin Pd membrane, leaving the thickness unspecified. All reported thickness data of electrolessly prepared Pd membranes seem to be estimated from the mass gain of the substrate  assuming a continuous deposit. The effective thickness depends on the membrane morphology, which  is  subsequently associated with  the  membrane  preparation  conditions  (Shu  et  al., 1994b).  The principal purpose of this work is to explore the possibility of the formation of ultrathin Pd films on mesoporous  ceramic  membranes.  Gas  permeation  behaviors through the prepared membranes were studied accordingly.

texts.orig: Matsuda and coworkers (1993) performed the dehydrogenation of  isobutane in  a  reactor  with  a  PdIAIA membrane. Their Pd film was coated on the outer surface of the substrate by electroless  plating, being dense and permselective toward hydrogen separation. The hydrogen permeation flux  through  the  fresh  membrane  was  measured  to  be 0.23 moVm2.s at 673 K under a pressure difference of  1 x lo5  Pa. When performing the dehydrogenation, they used a rotary  pump to evacuate  the permeated  hydrogen  from the permeation side in order to maintain a high permeation driving force. The isobutane conversion was found to be significantly beyond  the  equilibrium  values  observed  in  conventional (non-membrane) reactors at temperatures ranging from 623 to 723 K. The remaining problem was the membrane deactivation  due  to  coke  deposition on  the  membrane  surface which blocked the hydrogen permeation. It seems that this difficulty could be overcome by adding a small portion of hydrogen in the feed, though it would reduce the isobutane conversion. Zhu et al. (1993) tested  a 4-nm yA12q membrane  impregnated  with Cr2q in  performing  isobutane dehydrogenation. They examined the purge gas flow-rate effect,  and  found  that  the  isobutane conversion increased with the purge gas flow-rate. This was caused by the slightly enhanced hydrogen permeation rate through the membrane

Mesoporous yA1203  membranes  were  also  tested  in  nbutane  dehydrogenation by other  researchers  (Zaspalis et  al.,  1991b; Gokhale et  al., 1993).  Ioannides  and  Gavalas  (1993)  studied  isobutane dehydrogenation  in  a  membrane  reactor  with  a  CVDdeposited microporous SiOflycor glass membrane. Their membrane was declared to be quite permselective to hydrogen, with a selectivity  of Hz/N2 of  100 to 5000. It seems that gas permeation through this microporous membrane does not  follow  Knudsen  diffusion  mechanism.  A  somewhat enhanced isobutene yield was found when the dehydrogenation  was  performed  in  a  membrane-enclosed  reactor. Casanave et al. (1995) examined microporous zeolite membranes  in  performing  isobutane  dehydrogenation. A  substantial  enhancement of the isobutene yield was observed at 723  K  due  to  the  continuous separation of  hydrogen.  It seems that zeolite membranes have  still a  limited permeability at  elevated temperatures, say  573  to  873 K.  In  this paper, we  report  the application of ultrathin Pdlmesoporous ceramic  membranes  in performing isobutane dehydrogenation.

texts.orig: The starting ceramic membrane (denoted as SCT) was a porous alumina tube, commercially available from SCT-US Filter  (Bazet, France). The membrane tube had  an i.d. of 7 mm, an 0.d. of 10  mm and a length of 150 mm. It consists of a thin top layer of mesoporous yA1,q deposited on a support made of several aA12q  macroporous layers. Figure  1 shows schematically the membrane structure. The average pore size  and thickness  of each porous layer are listed in Table 1. The ceramic membrane tubes were used as substrate for composite palladium membranes without pretreatment. To facilitate the sealing of the membrane tube into the reactor, the two ends of the membrane were coated with fitted glaze over a 25 mm length.

texts.orig: Palladium was only plated on the inner surface (y-Al20J of the mesoporous  ceramic tube by electroless  plating while the  outer surface ( a - A 1 2 9 remained uncoated.  This prevented  the  possible  degradation  of  the  surface  film  by allowing free expansion  of the liquid in the mesopores upon heating the membrane. Prior to the deposition,  the substrate was  sensitized and activated with the conventional Pd-Sn activation  procedure (Shu et al., 1993). In order to ensure a uniform Pd seed spatial distribution, the activation procedure  was  conducted under  ultrasonic agitation. This was done  by  performing  the  activation  sequentially  in  two cylinders  containing  palladium  acetate  and  tin  chloride solutions, respectively,  in an ultrasonic bath  at  ambient temperature. Each dipping lasted for five minutes, and the successive dipping in P d 2 ' and Sn2+ solutions was repeated ten times. After the effective activation, palladium deposition was performed in an electroless solution designated as 'concentrated bath' in Table 2.  The detailed process was previously described by Shu et al. (1993). In order to plate palladium only on the inner surface, the ceramic tube was wrapped with Teflon tape, and not immersed in the deposition  solution.  The  electroless  solution  was circulated through  the  substrate tube  using  a  peristaltic  pump. Pd membranes SCTP-1 and SCTP-2 were thus prepared after depositions for 10 and 5 min, respectively. To improve the match of the thermal expansion between the ceramic substrate and the Pd film, a predeposition of Pd for  1 h in  a 'dilute bath (see Table 2) was practised, followed by  a deposition in the 'concentrated  bath' for another 1  h. It was found in our previous work (Shu et al., 1994b)  that isolated Pd particles would  be  deposited on the  substrate surface from the dilute bath. Such isolated  particles would act as an intermediate layer of  anchored nuclei to coat a  dense Pd film on the ceramic substrate using the concentrated bath. A Pd membrane denoted as SCTP-3 was prepared following  this  procedure. After  deposition, the  membrane  was washed  with  great  care,  and  dried  at  383  K  overnight. Deposited palladium contents were measured based on the mass gain of the substrate. The calculated Pd f i l m thicknesses of membranes SCTP-1, SCTP-2 and SCTP-3 were 0.72,0.48 and 2.1 p, respectively.  Table 3 gives the specification of these Pd membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both  nitrogen  and  hydrogen  were  used  as  permeation gases to evaluate the permeation behavior of the composite palladium membranes. Permeabilities were measured in the system shown in Figure 2. The membrane tube was installed in the reactor with Teflon ferrules or graphite seals depending on the operation temperature. This setup can be used to evaluate gas permeation behaviors through either porous or dense membranes. To sweep both sides of the reactor, either nitrogen  or  hydrogen  was  introduced  separately  to  the upstream  and the  downstream sides using  a multichannel mass flow controller. In the case of palladium membranes, hydrogen  was  introduced  to  the  reactor  at  temperatures higher than 573 K to prevent the Pd membrane from deformations due to the transition  between a-  and @forms  of palladium  hydrides.  The permeated  gas  flow-rate was  measured while setting a constant  pressure difference across the membrane and varying the pressure in the inner compartment of the reactor. The pressure difference was regulated by an automatic  valve connected  to a differential manometer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To carry out the dehydrogenation of isobutane, the membrane  reactor  should  resist  operation  temperatures  over 673 K. To this end, the membrane tube was installed in a reactor device with graphite seals sealing the tube ends of the  membrane.  The  configuration of  the  reactor  and  the reaction  test  apparatus  were  previously  described by Casanave et al. (1995). An electric heating tape was used to heat the reactor. The temperature gradient along the reactor length was controlled to be within *6 K.

texts.orig: A bimetallic Pt-lnlsilicalite catalyst was prepared by ion exchange following the method described by  Meriaudeau et al. (1995). The catalyst was shaped into pellets of about 2 mm in diameter and 2 mm long. About 2.25 g of the catalyst was packed in the inner compartment of the membrane tube with glassy beads and quartz fiber filling in the enamelled  ends  of  the  tube.  The  catalyst  bed  was  0.1  1  m  in height.

Then H2 (65 cm$TPymin) was introduced to the system to activate the catalyst.  After activation, the reactor was conditioned for dehydrogenation  test.
texts.text: For starting up, both sides of the membrane reactor were kept in a nitrogen  atmosphere.  The  heating  rate was controlled at  1 Wmin,  up  to  723 K.  Then H2 (65 cm$TPymin) was introduced to the system to activate the catalyst.  After activation, the reactor was conditioned for dehydrogenation  test.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A mixture of isobutane,  hydrogen and dilute gas nitrogen was fed into the inner compartment of the reactor for dehydrogenation.  The  feed  rate  of  the  mixture  was  about 53 cm@TP)/rnin  with 20 mol% of isobutane and 20 mol% of H2 A preliminary investigation showed that a portion of hydrogen around 20 mol% in the feed yielded a very positive effect on the stability and the selectivity of the catalyst due to  its inhibition of the  coke deposition on the catalyst (Bongondo, 1993). To sweep the permeated hydrogen, pure nitrogen was  introduced to  the  outer compartment as the purge  gas  with  a  flow-rate varying  from  60  to  300  cm3 (STP)/min. The dehydrogenation  was conducted at temperatures ranging from 673 to 773 K  at a negligible pressure difference across the membrane.

texts.orig: Permeation of  a  single  gas  through  macropores  (pore diameter dp > 50  nm)  or  mesopores  (2 < dp. < 50 nm) involves mainly two  transport mechanisms: viscous  flow and  Knudsen diffusion (Scott  and  Dullien,  1962; Keizer et al.,  1991). Their respective contributions depend on the pore radius and the mean free path of the gas molecule. If the pore radius is much smaller than the mean free path, the molecular  collisions  against  the  pore  wall  become  very important. This is the regime of Knudsen difiion. In contrast, if the pore radius is much larger th a n the mean free path,  the  molecule-wall collisions become  infrequent, the viscous  flow  remaining  predominant.  In  the  transition region, the two regimes of d i f i i o n   coexist. texts.orig: In the case of a mesoporous  membrane (2 < d < 50 nm), the contribution of the viscous flow, is negligibfe  when P, < 1 x lo6 Pa. Therefore, Knudsen diffusion is predominant at usual pressure. One should obtain a horizontal line of gas permeance  against the  average pressure. The  permeance results  obtained  with  the  SCT  ceramic  membrane  (see Figure 3) indicate this situation. After Pd deposition, the ceramic substrate surface was covered with an ultrathin Pd film. In this case, the permeation is mainly governed  by the resistance from the top metallic layer. The presence of a viscous flow behavior (Figure 3) indicates that the membrane has some defects such as cracks through which the viscous flow becomes important. In the case of composite Pdlceramic  membranes,  the  formation  of  cracks  is  not unexpected due to the mismatch of thermal expansion  coefficients (TEC) between the porous ceramic substrate and the  Pd  film  (the  TEC  difference being  in  the  order  of 7 @m.K).

texts.orig: To further demonstrate the hydrogen permeation enhancement at elevated temperatures, the  dependence of hydrogen permeance against the average pressure P , was examined at 773 K, as shown in Figure 6. With the increase of pressure in the inner compartment of the reactor, H2 permeation  first  increased,  reached  a  hump,  then  descended gradually. This uncommon permeation behavior should be considered as resulting from a combination of three diffision mechanisms: viscous flow through  defects, Knudsen diffusion through  pores and atomic diffusion through the dense part of the Pd film. As in the case of nitrogen permeation, the viscous flow induces an increasing permeance as the  average pressure  increased,  while  Knudsen  diffusion yields a constant flow-rate. For the atomic diffusion, though AP is constant, a simple calculation  indicates  that the driving force for atomic diffusion (PY5-PF5)  decreased with the increase of P , . The triple diffusion mechanisms result in a humped permeation curve.

texts.orig: two-step deposition was performed  successively from the dilute  bath and the concentrated bath (see Table 2). This was practised to avoid the difficulties associated with the mismatch  of  TEC  between Pd and  the  ceramic  substrate. According to our previous studies (Shu et al., 1994b),  crystallite Pd deposits preferentially formed fiom the dilute Pd bath.  The  isolated Pd particles acting as an intermediate layer were found to be effective in improving the match of the thermal expansion between  the Pd fi l m and  the  substrate. Figure 7 shows the N2 permeation behavior through the SCTP-3 membrane at the two stages of its preparation. Afier the deposition from the dilute bath, a f i l m of 0 . 4 pn (based on the mass gain) was obtained. At this stage, the membrane was still porous. After a second deposition of Pd from the concentrated bath, the resultant membrane SCTP-3 with  a total Pd thickness of about 2 pn w a s found to be dense with  no  measurable N2 permeation  flux  at ambient temperature. This is  however  the  thinnest Pd membrane reported to date based on our literature search.

The results are plotted  in  Figure  8 using  commonly accepted units  of  permeability  and  transmembrane pressure  difference in the form of square root. The permeability data did not show a horizontal line but rather a jump on each curve. Many researchers suggested to use pressure powers between 0.5 to  1 to describe the hydrogen permeation through th i n Pd f i l m s at  elevated  temperature  (Uemiya  et  al.,  1991b; Collins  and  Way, 1993;  Lewis, 1967).  However  the observed behavior in the present study could be related to a permeation  hysteresis phenomenon  which  was  discussed elsewhere (Shu et al., 1996).

texts.orig: was  obtained  with  a  closed  shell  side  (no  purge).  The obtained isobutane conversion was  somewhat higher than the equilibrium  value, which might imply a slight hydrogen permeation into the closed shell side, although there was no sweeping gas. In this case, the system was essentially like a conventional reactor which could  be  used  as a  reference. Upon purging the shell side of the reactor at a nitrogen flowrate of 250 cmYSTP)/min, isobutane conversion was significantly enhanced (Figure 9b). The same trend was obtained in the isobutene yield. This was also rather clear from the influence  of  purge  gas  flow-rate  on  the  conversion,  as shown in Figure 10.  Upon increasing the purge gas flow-rate up to 290 cm?STP)/min, the hydrogen separation efficiency through  the  membrane  was  increased  to  87.9%,  which resulted  in  an  isobutane conversion  increase  from  12  to 32.7%, and an isobutene yield from 8.8 to 30.8% at 723 K. The  influence  of  inlet  isobutane  concentration  was  also examined, as  shown  in  Figure  11. As  can  be  seen,  the obtained isobutane conversion was  twice  higher than  the equilibrium value. Therefore, the enhancement in both the isobutene yield and the isobutane conversion obtained upon purging (Figure 10)  was attributed to an enhanced hydrogen separation via the membrane.

texts.orig: ing into the reactor. The permeation flux of isobutane during dehydrogenation  was estimated to be at a small level of 8.6 x lo'  mol/m2.s. It would not change our general conclusion  that  isobutane  dehydrogenation was  significantly enhanced  by  hydrogen  separation  through  the  Pd  membrane. Practically,  performing the catalytic  reaction with the catalyst packed at the opposite side of the ultrathin Pd film might be more realistic. An alternative choice is to deposit the hydrogen permeable Pd in the porous framework of the support. It  is  the  pores  in  the  support that  constitute the transport  channels of  gas  permeation. Filling these  pores with Pd-based deposits can result in an effective hydrogen permeable  membrane.  Meanwhile,  the  holding  of  the deposit in the pores can no doubt improve the mechanical strength of the Pd membrane. The deposition of Pd inside the support pores can be done either by ion exchange in a similar way to the Pt  deposition (Uzio et al., 1993)  or by the via-pore electroless plating as practised in the fabrication  of VLSI (very large scale integration) chips (Ting, 1988).

texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, Pd was electrolessly deposited on the inner side surface of a mesoporous ceramic  tube using a peristaltic pump to circulate the electroless solution through the tube. Great care was exercised during the preparation to reduce defects formation due to the mismatch of thermal expansion coefficients between Pd and the ceramic substrate. A twostep deposition  procedure was effective in obtaining defectfree  membranes.  Permeation  behaviors  through  various membranes were  studied using hydrogen and nitrogen as permeation gases. In the case of Pd membranes  with cracks, the  gas  permeance  increases  with  the  average  pressure, showing a viscous flow behavior although the absolute per- meance value drops into the microporous range. A dense Pd membrane being  about  2 j u n thick  was  permselective to hydrogen. Isobutane dehydrogenation was performed  in a membrane reactor enclosed with  the  ultrathin Pdlceramic membranes. Upon purging the permeation side, enhanced dehydrogenation yields  were  obtained mainly  due  to  the increased hydrogen separation.
texts.text: In this study, Pd was electrolessly deposited on the inner side surface of a mesoporous ceramic  tube using a peristaltic pump to circulate the electroless solution through the tube. Great care was exercised during the preparation to reduce defects formation due to the mismatch of thermal expansion coefficients between Pd and the ceramic substrate. A twostep deposition  procedure was effective in obtaining defectfree  membranes.  Permeation  behaviors  through  various membranes were  studied using hydrogen and nitrogen as permeation gases. In the case of Pd membranes  with cracks, the  gas  permeance  increases  with  the  average  pressure, showing a viscous flow behavior although the absolute per- meance value drops into the microporous range. A dense Pd membrane being  about  2 j u n thick  was  permselective to hydrogen. Isobutane dehydrogenation was performed  in a membrane reactor enclosed with  the  ultrathin Pdlceramic membranes. Upon purging the permeation side, enhanced dehydrogenation yields  were  obtained mainly  due  to  the increased hydrogen separation.
